output_text:
Summary: Critique of the related-work positioning, with specific recommendations and a revised draft that tightens claims, adds missing literatures, and softens unverifiable novelty assertions.

## What's working

- Clear three-bucket structure (methods / descriptive / pre-LLM QC) that maps cleanly onto the contribution.
- Explicit gap statement at the end that names the design-space point being claimed.
- The instrument is described concretely enough (staggered platform rollouts) that a reader can locate the contribution.

## Problems with the current positioning

1. **Missing literatures that a reviewer will immediately flag.** The three buckets omit at least four bodies of work that sit directly adjacent:
   - *LLM contamination of crowdsourced data specifically.* Veselovsky et al. (2023, "Artificial Artificial Artificial Intelligence") estimated 33–46% of MTurk workers used LLMs on a summarization task. This is the single closest prior work and you must engage it — not just cite it under "descriptive."
   - *Data quality / model-collapse consequences.* Shumailov et al. on model collapse, Gerstgrasser et al. on accumulation, and the broader "training on synthetic data" literature establish *why* the quantity you estimate matters downstream. Without this, the motivation is asserted rather than grounded.
   - *Detection of LLM-generated text in worker outputs* (GPTZero-style classifiers, watermarking, stylometric detection). Reviewers will ask why you instrument rather than detect; you need a sentence acknowledging detection as the alternative identification strategy and why IV is preferable (detection error is non-classical and correlated with quality).
   - *IV designs using technology rollouts as instruments.* This is a well-trodden template (broadband rollout, smartphone rollout, ride-share entry). Citing 1–2 canonical examples buys you methodological credibility and shows the instrument isn't sui generis.

2. **The novelty claim is overreaching.** "First to deliver a credibly identified causal estimate" and "no prior work occupies this point" are exactly the sentences reviewers stress-test. Soften to "first IV design we are aware of" and let the contribution stand on the design rather than on a uniqueness claim.

3. **The instrument's validity is not previewed.** A related-work section that introduces an IV without flagging the exclusion restriction (platform rollouts affect annotation quality *only* through worker LLM use) leaves the most contested assumption to be discovered in Section 3. One sentence here pre-empts the obvious objection.

4. **"Annotation-fidelity scores" is undefined and load-bearing.** The outcome variable is mentioned twice without telling the reader whether it's a gold-standard comparison, a human rating, an automatic metric, or a downstream-model evaluation. The positioning paragraph is the wrong place to fully define it, but a parenthetical is warranted because each of those choices implicates different prior work.

5. **The pre-LLM QC paragraph is the weakest.** It currently says "these results don't speak to LLM contamination," which is true but uninformative. The interesting framing is that LLM use is a *new failure mode* that defeats several of those mechanisms (gold-standard injection in particular fails if the LLM also passes the gold items). Make that point.

6. **Tone drift.** "Genuinely new contribution" and "to our knowledge, the first" within two sentences reads as defensive. Pick one.

## Recommended revised draft

> We introduce CrowdCausal, an instrumental-variable design that estimates the causal effect of crowd workers' LLM use on the quality of text-summarization annotations entering downstream training pipelines. Our instrument is the staggered platform-level rollout of browser-based assistant integrations on Prolific and MTurk; the resulting LATE is identified on annotation-fidelity scores (defined in Section 2 as deviation from expert reference summaries).
>
> *Quasi-experimental methodology.* We draw on the standard panel toolkit — IV, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, synthetic control — together with recent corrections for staggered adoption and heterogeneous treatment effects (Callaway & Sant'Anna, Goodman-Bacon, de Chaisemartin & D'Haultfœuille). These methods are domain-agnostic and have not previously been applied with LLM exposure as the treatment. Our instrument follows a familiar template — technology-rollout IVs used in studies of broadband, smartphone, and platform entry — adapted to annotation labor.
>
> *Crowdsourcing in the LLM era.* A growing descriptive literature documents that crowd workers route tasks through assistants like ChatGPT, with Veselovsky et al. estimating that 33–46% of MTurk workers used LLMs on a summarization task. These studies establish prevalence and offer qualitative mechanisms, but do not identify a causal effect on output quality, and their measurements rely on self-report or detection classifiers whose error is correlated with the quantity being measured. We treat their prevalence estimates as a sanity check on our first stage rather than as identification.
>
> *Annotator quality control.* A pre-LLM literature studies how to raise annotation quality through incentive design, qualification tests, gold-standard injection, inter-annotator agreement, and adversarial filtering. LLM use is a failure mode several of these mechanisms were not designed for: gold-standard injection in particular loses power when the assistant passes the gold items, and inter-annotator agreement can rise rather than fall under shared LLM use. Our estimates speak directly to the size of this gap.
>
> *Downstream consequences.* The motivation for measuring this effect is the growing evidence that training on model-generated or model-contaminated text degrades downstream model performance (Shumailov et al.; Gerstgrasser et al.). Our LATE provides one of the first credibly identified inputs to that calculation on the annotation side of the pipeline.
>
> We are not aware of a prior IV design for LLM exposure in crowdsourced annotation, and Section 3 discusses the exclusion restriction — that platform rollouts affect annotation quality only through worker LLM adoption — in detail.

## Smaller line edits if you keep the original structure

- Replace "to our knowledge, the first design to deliver a credibly identified causal estimate" with "the first IV design we are aware of."
- Delete "genuinely new contribution"; the LATE either is new or isn't, the adverb doesn't help.
- "supplied to downstream training pipelines" → say what kind of pipelines (RLHF reward models? SFT data? evaluation sets?). Each implicates different audiences.
- "browser-based assistant integrations" — name them or footnote, otherwise the instrument is opaque.

## Uncertainty disclosure

I have not verified that Veselovsky et al. (2023), Shumailov et al., Gerstgrasser et al., or the staggered-DiD references (Callaway & Sant'Anna, Goodman-Bacon, de Chaisemartin & D'Haultfœuille) are the right citations for your specific framing — these are my recollections of the canonical work in each area and should be checked against the actual papers before inclusion. The Veselovsky 33–46% figure is from memory and should be verified. The claim that inter-annotator agreement can rise under shared LLM use is a plausible mechanism but I don't know of a published demonstration; treat as a conjecture to verify or remove.
